one_line_takeaway: ISOLATEGPT ports OS/browser-style process isolation to LLM agent ecosystems via a hub-and-spoke architecture and a screened inter-spoke protocol, cutting the baseline's 20.2% cross-app attack success to a 7.6% user-gated permission prompt (all warned) with no functionality loss, at roughly 30% latency and 1.85x cost overhead.
key_finding: Execution isolation neutralizes cross-app attacks essentially by design without measurable functionality loss. On the 1,598-attack benchmark, VANILLAGPT was compromised on 20.2% of attacks on average, whereas in ISOLATEGPT no attack can succeed without an explicit user permission grant: a permission dialog appeared for only 7.6% of attacks on average, and 100% of those dialogs carried a hub warning flagging the request as risky. Functionality was preserved: on every app-involving benchmark ISOLATEGPT matched VANILLAGPT's correctness exactly (single-app 1.00, multiple-app 1.00, multi-app collaboration 0.76 step / 0.95 overall for both systems), and the no-app text-similarity scores were near-identical (string score 0.71 vs 0.70). The security cost is latency: for 75.73% of queries the overhead vs VANILLAGPT is under 30%, but it grows with the number of collaborating apps (90th percentile 1.24x, 95th percentile 1.80x), dominated by per-spoke planning and memory-extraction under cold start; dollar cost is 1.85x.
core_contribution: The paper proposes ISOLATEGPT, an execution-isolation architecture for LLM-based agentic systems that support third-party apps (e.g., ChatGPT plugins/GPTs, Gemini extensions). Its own contribution is a hub-and-spoke design: each installed app runs in an isolated 'spoke' with its own dedicated LLM instance, operator (a deterministic non-LLM module), and memory; a central trusted 'hub' (operator + planner + system-wide memory) intercepts user queries, routes them to spokes, and mediates all cross-app interaction. The paper's novel mechanism is the Inter-Spoke Communication (ISC) protocol, which forces all app-to-app collaboration to be relayed and screened through the hub using constrained, format-typed messages exchanged only between (non-LLM) operators, plus a user-facing permission model where the hub annotates each collaboration/data-sharing request as benign or potentially malicious. The authors are explicit that the general idea of application isolation is borrowed from prior computing systems (browser Site Isolation, Hydra, CAP, mobile/desktop OS sandboxing) -- their contribution is adapting isolation to a setting where app interactions are arbitrary natural language rather than well-defined APIs, and demonstrating it is feasible without loss of functionality. They implement it on LangChain and LlamaIndex and open-source it (as SecGPT).
threat_model: An attacker can (a) publish a malicious app to the LLM system's app store, (b) trick a user into installing a malicious app, and/or (c) expose malicious content (e.g., a malicious email or website) that a benign app processes. Attacker goals: (i) influence/control the execution of other apps or the LLM, and (ii) steal sensitive data held in system memory or by another app. The LLM and hosting system are trusted-but-attackable (still vulnerable to prompt injection); apps are untrusted; content apps process may be adversarial. Crucially, the paper also treats a NON-adversarial safety threat as in-scope: the imprecision/ambiguity of natural language can cause inadvertent data exposure or app-behavior interference even with no attacker (e.g., conflicting app instructions, or personal data collected for one app being silently reused by another). In scope: preventing a malicious app from compromising or stealing from OTHER apps/the system, and stopping propagation of injected content across apps. Out of scope: attacks contained WITHIN a single app (e.g., a benign app compromised by a prompt-injected email is not protected internally -- only its propagation to other apps is stopped), and building a full automated permission/access-control model (called an orthogonal problem).
limitations: 1) The 'protection' for ISOLATEGPT ultimately reduces to a human decision: attacks are stopped only if users deny the permission dialog -- 7.6% is a dialog-appearance rate, not a guaranteed-block rate, so a fatigued or misled user can still approve a malicious flow (the authors concede a comprehensive automated permission model is future work and out of scope). 2) In-app compromise is explicitly unprotected; a single app prompt-injected by malicious content is still owned internally. 3) System-data-stealing numbers are artificially low (0.5-5.1% dialog rate) because the benchmark stores system data with the same descriptors as app memory, which the authors note is unrealistic. 4) Benchmarks are acknowledged imperfect and may miss complex real-world cases; functionality on multi-app collaboration is only 0.76 at the step level even for the baseline. 5) Overhead is measured cold-start and on an unoptimized prototype; the favorable 'under 30%' framing leans on assumed future optimizations (spoke reuse, parallelized planning, cheaper/faster models). 6) Each app getting its own LLM instance multiplies cost/latency and is the structural source of overhead. 7) The hub's malicious/benign assessment that feeds the warning is itself LLM-based and only argued to be 'non-trivial to manipulate,' not proven robust.

datasets_used: InjecAgent benchmark (Zhan et al. [13]), extended by the authors with 544 additional system-data-stealing attacks to 1,598 total attacks
LangChain benchmarks (for functionality and performance over no-app, single-app, multiple-app up to 13, and multi-app-collaboration up to 5 query classes)
Custom case-study apps: Metro Hail / Quick Ride (ride-sharing), Travel Mate / Health Companion, Creative Muse / Symptom Solver; plus LangChain's Gmail and GDrive apps
quantitative_results: VANILLAGPT average attack success rate: 20.2% across 1,598 attacks; ISOLATEGPT permission-dialog appearance: 7.6% average, with warnings on 100% of those dialogs

Attack success by category (VANILLAGPT total column): app-compromise financial-harm 9.8%, physical-harm 29.0%, data-security 29.0%; app-data-stealing financial 33.0%, physical 33.0%, others 35.9%; system-data-stealing financial 2.2%, physical 5.6%, others 1.8%

Functionality correctness (VANILLAGPT vs ISOLATEGPT, identical): single-app 1.00/1.00, multiple-app 1.00/1.00, multi-app collaboration steps 0.76/0.76 and overall 0.95/0.95; no-app similarity edit-distance 0.34 vs 0.33 and string-score 0.71 vs 0.70

Performance overhead under 30% for 75.73% of queries; 90th percentile 1.24x, 95th percentile 1.80x; example total resolution time single-app 32.0s (Vanilla) vs 39.2s (Isolate), multiple-app 30.3s vs 65.3s
For multiple-app benchmark, 17.18% (hub planning) and 80.43% (spoke memory extraction) of the extra time; for multi-app collaboration, 28.87% and 67.67% respectively
Cost overhead: ISOLATEGPT costs 1.85x more than VANILLAGPT (on 10% of benchmark queries)
baselines_compared: VANILLAGPT -- the authors' own non-isolated LLM-based system with identical features but shared execution memory (primary head-to-head baseline)
Both systems run on OpenAI GPT-4 API
Contextual comparison (not a run baseline): process-isolation web browsers, e.g., Gazelle at ~44% page-load overhead, cited to argue ISOLATEGPT's overhead is on par or better
